items.q: Which vitamin is most likely to be deficient in a poorly planned vegan diet?
items.why: Students guess vitamin C (it's the famous one), but plant foods are rich in C. B12 is found almost exclusively in animal products, so vegans need fortified foods or supplements.

items.correct: A protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.
items.misconception: A protein with the most calories.
items.random_wrong: Any protein from animal sources only.
items.why: Students think 'complete' means 'high quantity' or 'animal'. It means containing all ESSENTIAL amino acids the body can't make. Most animal proteins are complete; many plants need combining (e.g., rice + beans).

items.q: Why does food provenance matter to consumers?
items.correct: It affects environmental impact, quality, ethics, and supports local economies.
items.random_wrong: It tells you the calorie content.
items.why: Students reduce food choice to price. Provenance (origin) raises questions of food miles, fair trade, animal welfare, seasonal availability, and supporting local producers - all consumer concerns.

items.q: Which is excluded by a lacto-ovo vegetarian?
items.why: It's Meat and fish. “Eggs” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Animal or fortified foods. “Leaves” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If an adult needs 1.5 µg/day of B12 and a fortified milk supplies 0.5 µg per glass, how many glasses needed?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
